coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and antibacterial activity of silver@carbon nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Kunjie
Ji, Qingjuan
Li, Hongxia
Guan, Feng
Zhang, Deyi
Feng, Huixia
Fan, Haiyan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, hollow multiple-Ag-nanoclustes- C-shell nanocomposites (Ag@C) were synthesized by using silane coupling agent to graft carbon dots (CDs) with silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). CDs act as coating and stabilizing agent, protecting AgNPs from aggregation and oxidation. The resulting Ag@C nanocomposites demonstrate strong bactericidal effect against both gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria in the disk diffusion test. Cellular toxicity evaluation was performed using MTT assay. Meanwhile, the as-prepared Ag@C nanocomposites show a good biocompatibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silver@carbon
Nanocomposites
Antibacterial
Carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XPS survey spectrum of Ag@C nanocomposites. The inset is the Ag 3d spectra of the Ag@C nanocomposites.
FT-IR spectra of CDs and the Ag@C nanocomposites.
Raman spectra of the Ag@C nanocomposites.
SEM image of as-prepared Ag@C nanocomposites.
TEM image of as-prepared Ag@C nanocomposites.
The mapping image of Ag@C nanocomposites.
Bacterial effects of Ag@C against different biacteria (a, E. coli bacteria; b, S. aureus bacteria).
The cell viability of different concentration of Ag@C nanocomposites.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and antibacterial activity of silver@carbon nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Petrochemical Technology, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou 730050, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, School of Science and Technology, Nazarbayev University, Astana 010000, Kazakhstan.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Petrochemical Technology, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou 730050, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, hollow multiple-Ag-nanoclustes- C-shell nanocomposites (Ag@C) were synthesized by using silane coupling agent to graft carbon dots (CDs) with silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). CDs act as coating and stabilizing agent, protecting AgNPs from aggregation and oxidation. The resulting Ag@C nanocomposites demonstrate strong bactericidal effect against both gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria in the disk diffusion test. Cellular toxicity evaluation was performed using MTT assay. Meanwhile, the as-prepared Ag@C nanocomposites show a good biocompatibility.

Hollow multiple-Ag-nanoclusters- C-shell nanocomposites (Ag@C) were successfully fabricated using silane coupling agent to graft carbon dots (CDs) on silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). The nanocomposites have the advantages of both AgNPs and CDs, which perform efficient antibacterial activities against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus and exhibits little toxicity on hamster ovarian cells.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: With its strong inhibitory and bactericidal effects, as well as a broad spectrum of antimicrobial activities, the synthesis of AgNPs has been mature enough to be commercialized and regularly produced in industry [1–4]. However, the work has never been completed to improve its stability, dispensability, and efficiency by adjusting the nanostructures of various AgNPs [5,6]. Among many technologies and strategies in the development of functional AgNPs, much attention has been paid to protect AgNPs from aggregation and oxidation, and so Ag ions can be slowly released to the solution system. In such situation, AgNPs exhibit the best antibacterial performance. While efforts have been made and methodologies have been developed, the AgNPs encapsulated by mild materials are discovered as good candidates in the field of antibacterial activity [7–15]. Gao et al. have developed a simple and green approach to synthesize highly pure, well-crystalline and smooth-surfaced silver containing poly(N-isopropyl acrylamide (Ag@PNIPAM) with highly effective bioactive and antimicrobial activities [13]. Yang et al. explored an effective method to prepare nano-silver/sodium alginate (Ag/SA) composites, which restrained the aggregation of nano-silver and combined the advantages of nano-silver and SA [14]. The outer layers act as coating and stabilizing agent, which would allow silver ions slowly release to the environment, thereby producing and maintaining an excellent antibacterial effect [16]. CDs is one of the most environmental polymers with high photostability, low cytotoxicity and high biocompatibility. Recently, it has been widely applied to bioimaging, biosensing and biocatalytic fields [17,18].

The unique bactericidal effect of AgNPs and facile, low cytotoxicity feature of CDs inspired us to combine these two nanoparticles to make a new nanocomposite that demonstrate strong inhibition and bactericidal effect provided by AgNPs along with photostability, low cytotoxicity, and high biocompatibility offered by CDs.

In the present work, we designed a new approach to synthesize Ag@C nanocomposites by using silane coupling agent to graft CDs with AgNPs, and test the antibacterial activity and cellular toxicity of the materials.

KH550 was purchased from Shanghai crystal pure reagent Co., Ltd. Silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium citrate and ethanol were purchased from Tianjin fuyu chemical reagent Co., Ltd. All regents used were analytically pure (AR) and as received without further purification. De-ionized water was prepared with a Milli-Q-Plus system (18.2MΩ).

The morphology and dimension of the sample was revealed with JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) operating at 200 KV and JEOL JSM-6701F field emission-scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM). FT-IR spectra were taken on a Nicolet AVATAR 360 FT-IR spectrophotometer. Raman spectra were collected on an HR 800 Raman spectroscope (J Y, France) equipped with a synapse CCD detector and a confocal Olympus microscope. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) was investigated by a Shimadzu XRD-6000 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were generated by a PHI-5702 multifunctional X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (American). All optical measurements were carried out at room temperature under ambient conditions.

The AgNPs were prepared by the method reported previously [19]. Briefly, AgNO3, (90mg) was dissolved in 500mL of H2O and brought to boiling. Add 10mL 1% sodium citrate solution. The solution was kept on boiling for 1h. CDs were prepared using the method we reported previously [20]. The surface modification of AgNPs was carried out as follows: 0.2g of dried AgNPs was dispersed in solution of ethanol (20vol% water) and sonicated for 15min, then 0.03mL coupling agent was added to this system and sonicated for 10min; The mixture was filtrated and dried at 60°C. To prepare Ag@C nanocomposites, 0.02mg of CDs was dispersed in 5mL of ethanol (20vol% water) and sonicated for 10min. Meanwhile, 0.2mg of Ag-KH550 was re-dispersed in 25mL ethanol (20vol% water) and sonicated for 10min. The solution of CDs was then dropped into the Ag-KH550 suspension and the mixture was sonicated for 15min. The solvent was removed and the final product of nanocomposites were dried in vacuum oven at 80°C.

The effect of Ag@C nanocomposites for antibacterial activities against the S. aureus (gram-positive bacteria) and E. coli (gram- negative bacteria) was tested by the disk diffusion method [18]. The S. aureus and E. coli bacteria were grown on LB agar plates at 37°C to obtain the isolated colonies. The colonies were further dissolved in 0.85% saline solution. 100μL of the liquid bacterial colonies (105 CFUmL−1) was added to the Nutrient Broth Solid Medium previously treated with autoclave in culture dishes. The round disks (5.6mm in diameter) were soaked in different concentration of Ag@C nanocomposites (0.25, 0.5, 1mg/mL) for 10min. Finally, the round disks were placed onto the seeded agar plate. After 24h incubation at 37°C, the diameters of the inhibition zones were measured using Vernier caliper. The optical images were documented. Sterile water was used as control. The test was repeated three times.

The hamster ovarian cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 8000 cells/well for 18h. The cultural medium was then replaced by a serum-free medium containing various concentrations of Ag@C nanocomposites sample. Then, the solutions were replaced with 100mL serum free DMEM or 1640 containing 0.5mg/mL MTT and incubated for another 3h, the formazan crystals were dissolved in DMSO and the microplates were agitated for 3h at a medium rate prior to the spectrophotometrical measurement at the wavelength of 570nm on an ELISA reader (Model 680, Bio-Rad).

The untreated cells served as the 100% cell viability control and the completely died cells served as the blank. The relative cell viability (%) related to control cells was calculated by the formula below:
V % =
A experimental
−
A blank
/
A control
−
A blank
× 100 %

To modify the AgNPs, the hydroxyl group on the surface of AgNPs was replaced with OCH2CH3 on KH550 and bond to it. The organic chains in KH550 on the modified AgNPs provides steric hindrance that prevents AgNPs from aggregation. The amino group in KH550, on the other hand, can interact with the surface functional groups of CDs via hydrogen bonding. The formed Ag-KH550-CDs system can effectively shelter the active Ag from oxidation and corrosion. Such kind nanocomposite is endowed superior antibacterial activities and biocompatibility [23].

FT-IR spectrum of CDs (Fig. 3 ) before reduction show some broaden bands at around 3390, 1000, 1500(1396) and 1751cm−1 representing OH stretching, COC bending, CC and CO stretching vibrations in CDs respectively. The formation of Ag-KH550-C leads the OH stretching absorption broadening caused by the hydrogen bonding between KH550 and the CDs. CO stretching vibration becomes sharpened and shifted to lower frequency indicating the CO group on the surface of CDs participates the binding with KH550. In the CC stretching absorption region, the peak is sharpened and there appeared a new peak at the frequency 1324cm−1 (please find the frequency of the started peak), which can be potentially assigned to the characteristic vibration in KH550. The COC bending absorption is getting weak and also sharpened upon the formation of Ag-KH550-C composite. Overall, FTIR provides a solid evidence for the binding between the functional groups on the surface of CDs with the KH550 on the surface of AgNPs.

Raman spectra were recorded to further confirm the embedment of carbon in the composite. As shown in Fig. 4 , except for the peaks coming from Ag, two characteristics peaks located at about 1335 and 1607cm−1 are observed in the Raman spectra of Ag@C nanocomposites, corresponding to the D-band and G-band of carbon, respectively [8].

Fig. 5 shows a typical SEM image of the composites. It is clearly observed that small CDs attached to core of AgNPs. The detailed microstructures of composite were further examined by TEM, as shown in Fig. 6 . The average diameter of silver is about 80nm and the amorphous carbon shell is about 5nm thickness. The mapping image of the nanocomposites (Fig. 7a, b, c, d) also can evidence the CDs were coated on the Ag@C nanocomposites.

In the paper disk diffusion assay, the evident inhibition zones appeared for different concentration of Ag@C nanocomposites (0.25, 0.5, 1mg/mL) against both gram-negative E. coli bacteria (Fig. 8a) and gram-positive S. aureus bacteria (Fig. 8b). It could be concluded that silver ions from silver cores could be released through carbon shells owing to the entrance of the solution. It may be reasonably presumed that such core–shell structures may prolong the releasing time of Ag ions and thereby, preserving the sustained antibacterial behavior.

AgNPs or silver salts can cause damage to mitochondria, and DNA by increasing reactive oxygen species and leading to cell death [24]. CDs are proven to have good biocompatibility and have broad applications in biomedicine [25,26]. The in vitro cytotoxicity of the synthesized Ag@C nanocomposites was evaluated on hamster ovarian cells using MTT assay and the results was shown in Table 1 . When the concentration of nanocomposite is 0.5mg/mL, the cell viability was 96.03%. Even if the concentration increased to 1mg/mL, the cell viability still reached 77.49%. These results indicated that the obtained Ag@C nanocomposites have good biocompatibility. Furthermore, for silver and carbon nanocomposite, the toxicity to the mammal cells is similar to the result of MTT that is smaller than the ordinary AgNPs [27–29].

Monodispersed Ag@C nanocomposites were successfully fabricated using silane coupling agent to graft CDs on AgNPs. The results indicated that Ag@C nanocomposites demonstrate strong bactericidal effect on both gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria and great biocompatibility. Besides, the effective antibacterial concentration of Ag@C nanocomposites exhibits little toxicity on hamster ovarian cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grant National Natural Science Foundation of China (201664009), Universities research project of Gansu (2015A-041), the Hongliu young teachers cultivate project of Lanzhou University of Technology (Q201211) and the Doctoral research start-funded projects of Lanzhou University of Technology.
